attribute_description: Technology Readiness Level on the standard 1–9 scale (1 = basic principles, 9 = commercial deployment). Represents the maturity of a technology across its development lifecycle, with values progressing for non-mature technologies across projection years.

Aggregated maturity classification derived from Technology Readiness Level (TRL), with allowed values 'Prototype' (lab/demonstration phase), 'Emerging' (pilot/demonstration phase), and 'Mature' (commercially deployed). Updated per tech_year as per Nomenclature 4.2.

Overall energy conversion efficiency defined as the ratio of main output energy (kWh for electricity/heat, LHV for chemical fuels) to main input energy. Mathematically bounded between 0 and 1 (exclusive of 0, inclusive of 1). Reflects thermodynamic performance accounting for energy losses across conversion stages.

Dimensionless ratio representing the maximum possible efficiency (0 < η ≤ 1) calculated as the lower heating value (LHV) of main output energy divided by LHV of main input energy under ideal thermodynamic conditions. Defines engineering performance boundaries and operational limits for energy conversion technologies.

The expected operational lifetime of a technology, defined as the period over which the asset is expected to function without major refurbishment, based on engineering standards and accounting practices.

Fixed one-time capital expenditure independent of installed capacity. Represents a per-project lump sum cost in Euros (EUR), applicable to project-specific upfront investments not scaled with capacity. Engineering definition aligns with CAPEX categories excluding capacity-normalised costs.

Total upfront investment cost normalised per kW of installed power capacity (excluding land acquisition, grid reinforcement, and external infrastructure). Represents the primary scaling parameter for techno-economic analysis in ConvTech, with values constrained to non-negative real numbers (≥ 0 EUR/kW).

Fixed one-time operational expenditure independent of capacity. Represents non-recurring operational costs (e.g., initial setup, licensing, or one-off maintenance) normalised to the technology's functional unit. Expressed in Euros (EUR) per functional unit.

attribute_name: Fixed Operational Expenditure Percentage Of Capital Expenditure
attribute_description: Fixed annual operational expenditure expressed as a percentage of total capital expenditure (CAPEX), representing recurring costs (maintenance, insurance, administrative expenses) independent of variable factors like fuel or throughput. Mathematically bounded between 0% (no fixed OPEX) and 100% (CAPEX fully consumed by fixed costs annually). Engineering definition aligns with standard techno-economic analysis frameworks.

attribute_name: Annual Operational Expenditure Per Installed Capacity
attribute_description: Fixed annual operational expenditure per unit of installed capacity, excluding fuel costs. Represents normalized OPEX values ≥ 0, calculated as total annual fixed operational costs (maintenance, labor, insurance) divided by installed capacity in kW. Excludes variable costs, fuel, and depreciation.

Variable operational expenditure per unit of energy or mass output, excluding fuel costs. Expressed in EUR per kWh or EUR per kg, representing recurring annual costs for operation, maintenance, and consumables (excluding energy costs).

The characteristic operating temperature range for the core process, defined by engineering specifications and thermal integration requirements. This includes the typical temperature range during normal operation, accounting for process efficiency and material constraints.

The smallest assumed unit capacity for the technology, representing the minimum operational size. A value of 0 indicates no minimum constraint. This parameter defines the lower bound for installation capacity in engineering and economic assessments.
Qualitative uncertainty rating based on source methodology, bounding confidence intervals for techno-economic parameters (e.g., 'Low', 'Medium', 'High')

Technology-specific weighted average cost of capital (WACC) used to annualise capital expenditure over the technology's lifetime. Defines the discount rate applied to CAPEX for present value calculations in techno-economic analysis.

The reference unit size represents the nameplate capacity used as a basis for normalizing specific cost and performance data. It defines the operational scale for techno-economic parameters, ensuring consistency in calculations for CAPEX, OPEX, and efficiency metrics. The unit may be expressed in megawatts (MW) for power-based technologies or tonnes per hour (t/h) for mass-flow based technologies, depending on the technology's primary output carrier.

Specifies the energy or material carrier being stored, using abbreviations defined in the Carrier tab. Defines the type of carrier (e.g., electricity, hydrogen, methane) associated with storage technologies, enabling categorization and linkage to carrier-specific parameters.
Smallest assumed storage capacity in kWh, defining the lower operational bound for storage systems. Must be ≥ 0; 'nan' indicates no minimum constraint.

Dimensionless ratio representing the maximum charging power as a fraction of rated storage capacity, equivalent to C-rate in battery terminology. Defines operational limits for charging processes, accounting for thermal constraints, safety margins, and system efficiency degradation bounds.

Maximum discharging power as a dimensionless ratio of rated storage capacity, calculated as (nominal discharge power / rated storage capacity). Engineering bounds enforced between 0 (no discharge) and 1 (full discharge capability), with values derived from manufacturer specifications or system performance curves.

Ratio of energy input during charging to the energy stored, representing the efficiency of the charging process. Defined as a dimensionless value between 0 (no energy stored) and 1 (perfect efficiency), accounting for losses during energy conversion and storage.

Dimensionless ratio representing the proportion of energy output during discharging relative to energy input, bounded between 0 (no efficiency) and 1 (perfect efficiency). Quantifies system performance during energy release phases in storage technologies.

The minimum state of charge (SOC) represents the lowest allowable energy level as a fraction of the total storage capacity, ensuring safe and efficient operation. It is defined by engineering constraints, system design requirements, and operational safety margins.

The maximum state of charge (SOC) as a dimensionless ratio representing the upper limit of energy storage capacity relative to the total nominal capacity of the system, typically ranging from 0 (fully discharged) to 1 (fully charged).

Dimensionless quantity representing the rate of energy loss (self-discharge) per hour during standby mode. Math bounds: ≥0. Engineering definition: Derived from manufacturer specifications or empirical testing, representing fractional energy loss relative to stored energy. 0 indicates negligible loss.

Specific capital expenditure normalized per kWh of installed storage capacity, with a minimum value of 0. Expressed in EUR as per source currency specifications. Represents the primary scaling parameter for storage technologies in the StorTech dataset.

attribute_name: Annual Operational Expenditure Per Storage Capacity
attribute_description: Annual fixed operational expenditure normalized per unit of storage capacity, expressed in EUR per kWh of stored energy per year. Represents recurring non-fuel costs for maintenance, monitoring, and system operation. Must be non-negative value.

Cradle-to-gate GHG emissions for direct air capture systems, calculated via PREMISE v2.3.6 + ecoinvent v3.11 LCA framework, normalised to kgCO2eq per kg of captured CO₂, with geographic scope RER (Europe). Excludes operational and end-of-life emissions.
